text:
Competitive spinning tops may sound absurd but it’s one of the most popular toy brands in the world and it’s about to make a big comeback.

From Rubik’s Cube to Tamagotchi, history is not short of peculiartoysthat you would’ve sworn kids would never be interested in… until they become a multi-million dollar empire. Beyblade is a line of competitive spinning tops, which sounds like a joke until you learn it’s sold more than 160 million toys since 1999.

Like many toy lines, it at first seemed to be a fad. But interest never died off completely, particularly in its homeland ofJapan, and now creators Takara Tomy are preparing a relaunch of the brand worldwide. There’s to be a new anime series and new toy gimmicks but the core appeal remains the same: pitting spinning top against spinning top in a plastic arena, in a test of skill and luck which some fans take very seriously indeed.

Although the core audience is still children, those that were playing in the early 2000s now have kids of their own, as well as an urge to keep playing themselves. Nostalgia is key to the appeal of many toy brands nowadays and as it celebrates its 25th anniversary, Beyblade is poised to take full advantage of its legacy.

‘In the beginning it was solely geared towards children and for them to have fun as a toy,’ designer Kenji Horikoshi told Metro, during a visit to Japanese toy company Takara Tomy. ‘But as time went on more adults were able to find joy in playing Beyblade as well. And so that became more of a competitive feel.’

Before they merged with Tomy (who have their 100th anniversary this year), Takara were the original creators of The Transformers (which in turn is currently celebrating its 40th anniversary). For both toy lines they partner with American company Hasbro, who adapts them for the West and add their own designs and marketing twists.

‘As those kids became older, those that grew up with Beyblade are now adults and there’s this whole cycle of kids that are now interacting with Beyblade for the first time but alongside adults that also still play with Beyblade, which has led to it becoming naturally more competitive,’ explains Horikoshi.

In order to play Beyblade, all you need is a spinning top and launcher, and ideally a plastic *Beystadium* to drop them into – which has groves on the edges to encourage the tops to move around in a circle, as well as traps for the tops to fall into and put them out of play. To start, you simply place the top into the launcher and both players release them at the same time, at which point you just watch and see what happens.

It sounds as if there’s nothing to it, but that’s clearly not the case, given how good some players have got at it – with national tournaments organised across the global and international championship planned for later this year. What exactly these elite players are doing to perform so consistently I’m not sure, but no doubt it’s all in the wrist.

It brings to mind the famous British court case, in 1908, where it was proven that darts was not a game of chance and required skill to do well at, with an expert player of the day being asked to throw three 20s in a row in the courtroom. The Beyblade equivalent of this test is to play anyone that knows what they’re doing. Which is another way of saying I lost every battle I competed in.

You soon learn that preparation is key, with each top being composed of three separate components that are colour-coded for attack, defence, stamina, and balance. These can be mixed and matched however you want, to create a hybrid spinning top, although there is some complication when it comes to different generations of Beyblade, some of which have overpowered parts.

In tournament battles this means the adjudicators have to carefully examine each top before it’s allowed to compete, as they adjust the scoring system so that one built with overpowered parts has a points handicap. One of the competitors we watched risked instant failure, by using a very overpowered top, so that if he lost even one round he was out, but few were that brave.

I was invited to watch a local tournament and it was fascinating to see the split between younger and older players, all competing together in apparent harmony. Although it was the kids that were showing the most obvious signs of excitement. One girl, who couldn’t have been more than six years old, joined other younger elements of the crowd in screaming the phrase ‘3, 2, 1, Go Shoot!’ that proceeds each battle.

All the kids were cheering on the players as if watching their favourite sports team, which is exactly the sort of attitude Takara Tomy want to encourage.

For a long while though, there hasn’t been any physical tournaments, thanks to Covid, which created a major problem for Beyblade, since it’s very much an in-person social experience. Horikoshi explains that Takara Tomy were forced to experiment with online tournaments and that, surprisingly, it proved very popular.

Teaming up with YouTubers specialising in Beyblade they’ve also expanded to other online arenas, with the official Beyblade section of Roblox seeing 4.5 million visitors during lockdown. In Japan there’s even been cross-promotion with one of Tokyo’s biggest baseball teams.

‘The pandemic was really rough for everyone, but fans were able to really understand and appreciate the Beyblade community and with tournaments making a comeback a lot more fans are becoming a lot more engaged and coming to these areas where they can interact with each other,’ says Horikoshi.

That forced push into embracing online has worked out for the best, with the tactile pleasures of the game now augmented by an app that has been downloaded 50 million times in Japan. Indeed, you could see many of the players at the tournament with a strange device on their launcher that I initially took to be some sort of motorised ripcord but was in fact a stat tracker to connect to the app, to study details like the exact speed and duration of a spin.

Beyblade is serious business for some people, but the fandom extends beyond just the toys, to highly successful manga and anime series as well. These parallel the changes in the toy line, with Takara Tomy already having launched the fourth generation in Japan and Hasbro recently following suit in the West.

Perhaps the most surprising thing about Beyblade is that it’s not actually that expensive. You can get everything you need for a battle for less than £50, although of course the more you get into it the more you’re likely to spend on additional spinning tops, to mix and match their parts.

That green stripe is the X-Celerator Rail (Hasbro)

Beyblade X Xtreme Battle Set– £44.99This is the obvious place to start as it includes two fourth generation Beyblade X tops, a Beystadium with the X-Celerator rail, and two launchers with ripcords. That’s all you need to play and while only having a single top each means you’ll be short on pre-match strategy options this is a cheap way to see whether you and your kids enjoy the concept.

Ripcord or string are the two big launcher choices (Hasbro)

Beyblade X Soar Phoenix Deluxe String Launcher Set– £19.99If you start getting into Beyblade then this is the next obvious step, as it includes a new top and an alternative string launcher. Which launcher you use seems to be a matter of preference but neither option gives an unfair advantage.

The colours are very important (Hasbro)

Starter Pack Sets– £9.99 eachExtra spinning tops are sold separately and have names like Arrow Wizard 4-80B and Scythe Incendio 4-60T. No matter the generation they can all be taken apart and put together again in any combination, with the three separate parts each being colour-coded according to whether they’re designed for attack, defence, stamina, and balance.

The new gimmick for the fourth generation is ‘super acceleration’ on all the new spinning tops, which is enabled by the new X-Celerator rail on the Beystadium – a toothed railing along the perimeter of the arena that can speed up tops even faster.

At the same though, there’s an attempt to feed into the nostalgia of the 25th anniversary, with many of the older sets and gimmicks also being brought back. A new generation also means a new anime series, called Beyblade X, which will come to UK screens later this year.

‘We were very confident with our toys and having that face-to-face interaction. Although there is this metaverse digital element, that is present in the modern day, we’re confident that we’ll continue to innovate them both. So, we’ll have the digital element and also the physical element, and that is going to be the new formula as we continue to grow.’

More than once, we hear Takara Tomy and Hasbro staff joke about making Beyblade an Olympic sport, but it’s clear that there’s at least a kernel of honest hope that it could one day happen… perhaps for the 50th anniversary. At the very least, they’re open about their ambitions for Beyblade to be accepted alongside other ‘gear-assisted’ sports like golf and skateboarding.

‘Our mission here is to make sure we continue innovating and as the years progress, even without it being an Olympic sport, we just want to make sure people globally can have a chance to really appreciate and play with each other and have that competitive feel.’

Spinning tops as a serious sport may seem absurd but to a child it’s conceptually no different to darts or golf. But whether it gets mainstream recognition or not it doesn’t really matter, as Beyblade’s success has been evident for decades now and as Beyblade X puts a new spin on things, it’s attempting to attract a bigger audience than ever.

title: Beyblade returns - how spinning tops could become an Olympic sport | Metro News
description: The most unlikely toy sensation of the 2000s is back, as the spinning tops of Beyblade X return with a new anime and Olympic level ambition.
